notes: The "fish gate" was on the north end of the city, and those there would be the first to see an enemy invading from the north. The fish gate opened into the part of the city known as the "second quarter" (v. 10), probably because it was an expansion of the original city of David. This quarter would be the first reached from the north. (Keil and Delitzsch, Commentary, 10:2:134).
"Maktesh" was the name of the merchant's quarter, which lay in the second quarter; thus, the reference to merchants, "they that bear silver. (Keil and Delitzsch, Commentary, 10:2:134).

"Settled upon their lees" (v. 12) is a figure drawn from wine making. The lees are the thick residue of the pulp of the grapes. "Good wine, when it remains for a long time upon its lees, becomes stronger; but bad wine becomes harsher and thicker" (Keil and Delitzsch, Commentary, 10:2:134).

To "search with candles" suggests an exhaustive search, since in the poorly lighted houses of those times one would have to use a candle to look for a lost object at night. (Keil and Delitzsch, Commentary, 10:2:134).

We must not underestimate, however, the difficulty of the last days. Joel and Zephaniah both speak of the last days as being “a day of … gloominess” (Joel 2:2; Zephaniah 1:15). The coming decades will be times of despair. Why? Because, as Moroni said, despair comes of iniquity. (See Moroni 10:22.) The more iniquity, the more despair. And unless there is widespread repentance, despair will both deepen and

quotes.text: And the Prophet Zephaniah interprets the way of the Lord: [quotes 1:12-15]
quotes.speaker: Spencer W. Kimball
quotes.attr: Spencer W. Kimball, in Conference Report, Apr. 1952, 22; or Improvement Era, June 1952, 418

quotes.text: The word translated here as nation is the Hebrew goi; it is a generic word meaning "any or all people." Thus this chapter, which contains condemnations of various peoples other than Israel, begins with an invitation to people not before chosen ("desired") to respond to the call of Abraham and "seek . . . the Lord . . . seek righteousness, seek meekness" (Zeph. 2:3). It was an invitation little heeded before Babylon's invasion but one that will be heard and implemented in the days before the fall of spiritual Babylon.
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 673
